title: New Hampshire

jokes.text: Ben: 'Am I dreaming? Do I have my pants on?'
jokes.setup: News reporters are breathlessly announcing Selina is about to become President of the United States — a moment of historic gravity.
jokes.punchline: Ben's first reaction to the historic moment is to check whether he's wearing pants, instantly deflating the grandeur.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ben characterization — cynical, world-weary, physically disheveled. The pants joke immediately establishes his exhausted detachment from historic gravity. Economy is superb: two short questions do the work of a paragraph. Strong character integration because only Ben, the perpetually harried chief of staff, would react this way.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Ma'am, I don't wanna piss on your bliss, but...'
jokes.setup: Selina is euphoric about becoming president.
jokes.punchline: Ben uses the phrase 'piss on your bliss' — a deliberately crass rhyming euphemism — before immediately pivoting to campaign logistics.
jokes.explanation: Solid Veep-style vulgarity with a rhyming twist. The phrase is memorably crude and very in-character for Ben. Slightly cheaper than the pants joke because the rhyme is a bit of a pun crutch, but it flows naturally.

jokes.text: Mike yells 'President of the United States!' after pretending to forget what Selina is becoming.
jokes.setup: Mike acts as if he genuinely can't remember what Selina's big news is, stringing her along.
jokes.punchline: He yells 'PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES!' — the comedic beat is his performative amnesia followed by explosive enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: Functional crowd-pleaser. The fake forgetfulness before the eruption is a classic comedic beat — not original but executed with energy. Mike's doofus-sincere character suits it.

jokes.text: Selina: 'She's calling me a Single Lady.' (about Beyoncé's voicemail)
jokes.setup: Dan reveals Beyoncé left Selina a voicemail congratulating her. Someone asks how Beyoncé got the number.
jokes.punchline: Selina ignores the question and instead gleefully interprets the contact as Beyoncé calling her a 'Single Lady' — referencing the song while being obliviously self-congratulatory.
jokes.explanation: Clever double-meaning — the song title fits Selina (divorced, newly powerful) and her euphoric self-absorption in the moment lands the joke. Good character integration showing Selina's vanity. Pop culture reference but with a genuine twist.

jokes.text: Amy: 'I'm not sure that Beyoncé fully understands that.' (about Selina still needing to campaign while being president)
jokes.setup: Amy tries to bring the team back to earth — Selina is still campaigning to BE president while already becoming president.
jokes.punchline: Amy's dry understatement that Beyoncé doesn't understand the procedural nuances of simultaneous presidential ascension and primary campaigning.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Amy deadpan — she's the only one trying to do her job while everyone fangirls over Beyoncé. The absurdity of criticizing Beyoncé for not understanding electoral politics is the joke, delivered with Amy's characteristic exasperation.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Holy shit. It's like Lolla-fucking-palooza.' / 'It's like I'm a Beatle.' Staff member: 'Well, you got the haircut for it.'
jokes.setup: Selina is overwhelmed by the cheering crowds greeting her.
jokes.punchline: A staff member agrees she's like a Beatle — then adds the real punchline: 'you got the haircut for it,' a subtle dig at her appearance.
jokes.explanation: The Beatles setup is fine, but the haircut punchline is what elevates it — it's a diss disguised as an agreement, and the audacity of saying it to the president-elect is very Veep. Clean structure.

jokes.text: Dan: 'From Ashton Kutcher's assistant, Ashton says congrats.' Selina: 'Assistant?'
jokes.setup: Celebrity well-wishes are rolling in. Dan reads a message from Ashton Kutcher.
jokes.punchline: Selina's single-word deflation — 'Assistant?' — she's insulted that the message came via proxy rather than directly from the celebrity.
jokes.explanation: One of the sharpest jokes in the episode. Maximum economy — one word does all the work. It perfectly encapsulates Selina's narcissism: in the moment she's becoming the most powerful person in the world, she's wounded that Ashton Kutcher didn't personally contact her. The absurdity of the priority hierarchy is the joke, and it's entirely earned by character. Justifies the 8+ because it's comparable to 'Never go with a hippie to a second location' in economy and character specificity.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Goodbye, China.' (after learning Selina will acquire nuclear codes)
jokes.setup: Sue announces Selina will acquire nuclear codes after the oath.
jokes.punchline: Ben's deadpan 'Goodbye, China' — implying Selina will accidentally nuke China.
jokes.explanation: Two words, devastating implication. Ben's complete lack of faith in Selina's judgment, delivered with zero drama, is perfectly in character. The dark comedy of a chief of staff waving goodbye to an entire nation upon hearing his boss will have nuclear codes is inspired. High quotability — this line travels well out of context.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Is Jack Ruby still alive?' (after suggesting they 'take out' political rivals)
jokes.setup: Selina says 'God, can't we just take 'em out?' about her political rivals.
jokes.punchline: She immediately asks if Jack Ruby — the man who assassinated Lee Harvey Oswald — is still alive, escalating from political hyperbole to actual assassination reference.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'take 'em out' to a specific historical assassin is the joke — Selina's power hunger taking an immediate dark turn. The specificity of Jack Ruby (not just 'a hitman') adds an educated absurdism. Strong structure: casual suggestion → specific murderer-for-hire reference.

jokes.text: Selina: 'God, there's so many people in here. It's like a Mormon orgy.'
jokes.setup: The presidential staff is merging with Selina's existing staff, creating chaos.
jokes.punchline: Selina compares the overcrowded room to a 'Mormon orgy' — an oxymoron that implies both size and chaos.
jokes.explanation: Funny oxymoron — the 'Mormon orgy' image is absurd enough to land. Selina's casual irreverence is well-characterized. Slightly cheap in that it's a stock 'shocking combination' joke, but the delivery context saves it.

jokes.text: Selina: God, there's so many people in here. It's like a Mormon orgy.
jokes.setup: The presidential staff is merging with Selina's campaign staff, creating chaos.
jokes.punchline: 'Mormon orgy' — a contradiction in terms that perfectly captures an overwhelming crowd without the normal connotations of an orgy.
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable non-sequitur that's very Selina — the oxymoron is perfect. 'Mormon orgy' suggests volume without sex, which is exactly the point. Strong image economy.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Here, boy. Come to me.' (Selina calling for Ben like a dog)
jokes.setup: Selina calls 'Ben? Ben?' searching for him in the crowded room.
jokes.punchline: Ben responds 'Yeah?' and Selina says 'Here, boy. Come to me.' — treating her chief of staff like a pet.
jokes.explanation: Power-dynamic humor that runs throughout Veep. Selina's instant comfort with presidential authority extended to treating staff like animals. Functional but not original.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Do you want me to tell you which of those two to fire?' / 'Kent. Just kidding, Kent.'
jokes.setup: Selina has dismissed most people; only Ben and Kent remain.
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately offers to tell Selina which of the TWO people left in the room to fire, then names Kent directly, then backtracks with 'Just kidding, Kent' — suggesting it might not be a joke.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: the absurdity of offering to advise on firing when there's only one candidate, and the barely-veiled hostility between Ben and Kent. 'Just kidding, Kent' landing with zero warmth is the real punchline.

jokes.text: Ben reveals he was 'bulimic the whole first year' as Chief of Staff 'and didn't even lose any weight from it.'
jokes.setup: Selina is asking Ben to be her acting Chief of Staff, noting the job 'destroyed' him.
jokes.punchline: Ben shares that being Chief of Staff made him bulimic — then immediately undercuts the horror with 'and I didn't even lose any weight from it,' the ultimate dark comedy beat.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is near-perfect: dark revelation (bulimia from job stress) + deflating twist (didn't even lose weight) = layered comedy. It's deeply Ben — physically slovenly, emotionally wrecked, self-deprecating about serious mental health struggles in a completely matter-of-fact way. Selina's response ('No, you weren't. None of that is true.') adds another layer by suggesting she simply refuses to accept his trauma. Highly quotable out of context. Comparable to the calibration anchors for 8+ work: specific, character-integrated, surprising structure.

jokes.text: Selina: No, you weren't. None of that is true. What are you talking about?
jokes.setup: Ben delivers the shocking bulimia confession.
jokes.punchline: Selina's flat denial — not even processing what she's hearing — because she's too focused on getting what she wants. The reaction is funnier than engaging with it.
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Selina doesn't engage with Ben's trauma at all — her dismissal ('None of that is true') is both absurd and character-perfect. Works as a topper to joke 14.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I am 10 centimeters dilated. I'm fully effaced. I mean, this presidency is crowning. I need you.'
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to convince Ben to be her Chief of Staff, reminding him he said he'd 'midwife' her through the campaign.
jokes.punchline: She extends the childbirth metaphor to its fullest, clinical extreme — dilation, effacement, crowning — applying OB-GYN terminology to presidential politics with total commitment.
jokes.explanation: The escalating commitment to the metaphor is the joke — Selina doesn't just nod to the midwife reference, she goes all the way to 'fully effaced' and 'crowning.' The absurdism of discussing obstetric terminology in a political context with complete sincerity is very Veep.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Ben, I swear to sweet Jesus Christ, if you don't do this, I'm gonna bring back Prohibition.'
jokes.setup: Selina is desperately trying to get Ben to agree to be Chief of Staff.
jokes.punchline: Her ultimate threat is to bring back Prohibition — an absurd, unenforceable presidential power flex that reveals her desperation and also her awareness of Ben's relationship with alcohol.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant on multiple levels: (1) it's a presidential threat that's both absurd in scale and personally targeted at Ben's drinking, (2) it shows Selina immediately thinking about how to weaponize presidential power for petty personal leverage, (3) 'sweet Jesus Christ' as a blasphemous oath before threatening Prohibition is a perfect comedic rhythm. Highly quotable. The character integration — targeting BEN's specific vice — pushes this to 8+.

jokes.text: Male reporter: 'He's so Washington, his blood type is DC.'
jokes.setup: News coverage describes Ben Cafferty continuing as Chief of Staff.
jokes.punchline: The pundit quip: 'He's so Washington, his blood type is DC' — DC as both the location and blood type abbreviation.
jokes.explanation: Functional wordplay — the DC blood type pun is clever enough but feels like pundit filler. Low character integration since it's a generic talking-head observation. Gets a chuckle but doesn't punch above its weight.

jokes.text: Amy: 'Thank you for interrupting your studies, your start-ups, your retirements, your federal sentences.'
jokes.setup: Amy is rallying the campaign team.
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from respectable interruptions (studies, start-ups, retirements) to 'your federal sentences' — implying some volunteers are literally serving prison time.
jokes.explanation: Classic list-escalation comedy with excellent structural execution. The fourth item in the list is the unexpected turn that lands the joke. Amy delivering it with complete sincerity, as if 'federal sentences' is a perfectly normal item on the list, is the deadpan that sells it. Good economy.

jokes.text: Mike: 'This is an historic moment. I'm not talking about this first female president thing. I'm talking about the first redhead press secretary.'
jokes.setup: The team is discussing the historic moment of the first female president.
jokes.punchline: Mike reframes the historic significance entirely around himself — specifically his red hair — treating his own appointment as more significant than the first female president.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Mike: oblivious, self-important, and genuinely convinced his hair color is historically significant. The structure is clean — acknowledging the real historic moment only to immediately dismiss it in favor of his own trivial milestone. High character integration; this line belongs to Mike alone.

jokes.text: Mike: 'Big crowd today. Something important happen that I'm missing?' (entering the White House briefing room)
jokes.setup: Mike enters the crowded White House press briefing room for the first time as press secretary.
jokes.punchline: He pretends not to know why there's a big crowd — feigning unawareness of the historic moment for comic effect, playing to the press corps.
jokes.explanation: Competent Mike-being-Mike moment. The joke is self-deprecating in a way that lands mildly. Not especially original — the 'oblivious to obvious thing' bit is well-worn — but fits the character.

jokes.text: Mike: 'Any questions? You know what, I'll do the first one myself. Uh, Mike McLintock, why are you so darn handsome?' (press corps laughs) 'Uh, why are you laughing at that one?'
jokes.setup: Mike opens the floor for questions at the press briefing.
jokes.punchline: He asks himself a softball question about his own handsomeness. The press corps laughs — presumably at the absurdity. Mike then asks why they're laughing, revealing he meant it sincerely.
jokes.explanation: The cringe escalates beautifully: Mike's softball self-question would be mildly funny on its own, but the press laughing AND him asking why they're laughing — implying he genuinely thought it was a serious question — makes it a two-beat cringe gem. Strong character integration: Mike's hapless self-regard and total lack of self-awareness.

jokes.text: Dan refers to Kelly and Richard Splett as 'Dynamic Duo — Batman, Boy Wonder... young... Lady Wonder.'
jokes.setup: Kelly and Richard Splett have arrived to help the campaign.
jokes.punchline: Dan starts the Batman reference confidently, realizes Kelly is a woman, and awkwardly pivots to 'Lady Wonder' — a non-existent DC character — mid-sentence.
jokes.explanation: The joke is Dan's condescension revealing itself mid-metaphor. The hesitation before 'Lady Wonder' is the real comedic beat — implied from the ellipses. Moderately funny but not one of the episode's standouts.

jokes.text: Richard Splett: 'I'll hit the phones until I have a sore throat and a hot ear.'
jokes.setup: The team says they should 'hit the phones.'
jokes.punchline: Richard Splett responds with his signature over-literal, over-enthusiastic sincerity: he will phone-call until his body physically suffers — sore throat, hot ear — which is an absurdly specific and earnest commitment.
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Splett — excessively literal, weirdly specific, and earnest to the point of absurdity. 'Hot ear' is a specific physiological detail that makes the joke. Strong character integration for what is still a minor character in this episode.

jokes.text: Dan Egan: 'I bring bad news to the closet.' (meeting held in a closet)
jokes.setup: An aide asks Dan why he called a meeting in a closet.
jokes.punchline: 'I bring bad news to the closet' — Dan has a designated location for delivering bad news, and it's a closet, which he states with total casualness.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Dan having a closet reserved for bad news is darkly funny. It implies this happens so often he's developed a system. Economy is excellent — it's six words that suggest an entire pathological management style.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Give you 24 hours? Like in a movie?' / 'No, I mean, yes. Please.' / 'You have six hours. We can't afford 24.' / 'Or for six hours, depending on how you do.' / 'Alternatively, goodbye forever.'
jokes.setup: Dan must resign over the Chung torture rumor leak. He asks for time to find a solution.
jokes.punchline: The aide's responses are arid and precise — he can't even commit to the movie cliché of 24 hours, gives him six, then undercuts every expression of good faith with a potential goodbye.
jokes.explanation: Classic Iannucci dialogue — every beat of apparent warmth is immediately qualified into coldness. 'Like in a movie?' is a sharp bit of meta-awareness. 'Alternatively, goodbye forever' as a parting shot is perfect.

jokes.text: Boss: Give you 24 hours? Like in a movie? / Dan: No, I mean, yes. Please. / Boss: You have six hours. We can't afford 24.
jokes.setup: Dan begs for time to fix the Chung torture rumor problem.
jokes.punchline: The boss deflates the movie-cliché '24 hours' to six, with the bureaucratic logic that they can't afford the Hollywood version.
jokes.explanation: Meta-awareness of political thriller clichés, executed efficiently. 'We can't afford 24' deflates genre conventions with bureaucratic logic.

jokes.text: Mike: 'They fuckin' love me in there, Ben. My bit about gun control killed. My bit about voter ID laws identified itself as hilarious.'
jokes.setup: Mike returns from a press briefing he conducted on no sleep.
jokes.punchline: 'My bit about voter ID laws identified itself as hilarious' — Mike uses 'identified itself' as a play on 'ID' while describing his own comedy with delusional confidence.
jokes.explanation: The voter ID pun ('identified itself as hilarious') is a clever embedded wordplay that Mike probably doesn't realize he's making. His delusion about his press briefing comedy act is both funny and painful. Strong character work.

jokes.text: Ben: 'You know, I told you when I was telling you I wasn't telling you what I told you. The fuck stops here, Dan.'
jokes.setup: Dan confronts Ben about telling him the Chung torture story. Ben denies it.
jokes.punchline: Ben's denial is a bureaucratic tautology — 'I told you when I was telling you I wasn't telling you what I told you' — followed by a profane twist on 'the buck stops here.'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional political doublespeak comedy. The circular logic of 'I told you when I was telling you I wasn't telling you what I told you' perfectly parodies Washington deniability culture — it sounds coherent but means nothing and everything simultaneously. Then 'The FUCK stops here' as a corruption of Truman's phrase is sharp political satire with precise economy. Justifies 8+ because it's original, structurally crisp, and deeply character-specific to the Washington insider milieu.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'So I'm gonna go to Africa and help install sewage systems in poor communities or whatever. Or go to Wall Street, I don't know. One of the two.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is telling Dan he's done with DC.
jokes.punchline: He presents African humanitarian work and Wall Street as equivalent options he hasn't decided between yet — the moral equivalence is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jonah — zero self-awareness about the moral gap between NGO work and financial sector profiteering, presented with complete casualness. 'Or whatever' after 'sewage systems in poor communities' is the dismissive beat that sells it. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'But, Dan... I've never told a lie in my life.' Dan: 'That was a lie. Try to keep up, Egan.'
jokes.setup: Dan asks Jonah to lie to the press about the Chung torture rumor origin. Jonah objects.
jokes.punchline: 'I've never told a lie in my life' is itself an obvious lie. Dan immediately points this out with 'That was a lie. Try to keep up, Egan.'
jokes.explanation: A classic logical paradox joke executed with perfect brevity. Dan's 'Try to keep up, Egan' adds condescension that elevates it beyond the obvious. The structure is tight and the delivery implied is sharp.

jokes.text: Dan's phone call: 'I would love to go for margaritas. Just to warn you, though... I do like to lick the rim.'
jokes.setup: Dan takes a phone call from 'Red-hot Rachel from the Speaker's Office' while in the middle of a serious conversation with Jonah.
jokes.punchline: 'I do like to lick the rim' — an innuendo that applies equally to margarita glasses and sexual activity, delivered with Dan's signature casual sleaziness.
jokes.explanation: The innuendo itself is fairly stock, but the context — Dan pivoting from political crisis management to leisurely flirtation — is perfect character work. The ease with which he code-switches reveals his priorities.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'Three-pack-of-Kleenex Rachel? Yeah, I know Rachel.'
jokes.setup: Dan mentions Rachel Marsh from the Speaker's Office as a perk of the West Wing.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's nickname for her — 'Three-pack-of-Kleenex Rachel' — implies he's used three boxes of tissues while thinking about her, an extremely vulgar implication delivered with Jonah's earnest cluelessness.
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly economical — three words that say everything. The fact that Jonah has a tissue-specific nickname for a woman implies both his objectification AND his pathetic isolation. Deeply Jonah. Very quotable as a euphemism.

jokes.text: Kent: I don't want you on top of me. / Dan: Well, I'm going to be. So, work with it.
jokes.setup: Kent and Dan argue about who gets the office closer to the Oval Office — the physical proximity debate becomes an inadvertently sexual metaphor.
jokes.punchline: Dan's 'Well, I'm going to be. So, work with it' — delivered as if the sexual subtext doesn't exist — turns the bureaucratic dispute into something genuinely uncomfortable.
jokes.explanation: The inadvertent double entendre works because neither character seems aware of it, which is funnier than if they played it consciously.

jokes.text: Kent and Gary fighting over office space in the West Wing, with Gary arguing 'That's my calculator' and Kent: 'You don't need an office. You have a bag.'
jokes.setup: Gary and Kent are both claiming the same West Wing office.
jokes.punchline: Kent's devastating dismissal: 'You don't need an office. You have a bag.' — reducing Gary's entire professional identity to his man-bag.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy. Kent's characterization of Gary as a person whose entire function is contained in a bag is both precise and devastating. The calculator detail ('That's my calculator') is a nice setup — Gary's professional claim is a calculator, which only makes Kent's retort land harder.

jokes.text: Selina to her daughter Catherine: 'Couldn't have done it without ya.' Catherine: 'What have I done to help?' Selina: stammering non-answer.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Catherine she couldn't have done it without her, in a moment of apparent mother-daughter warmth.
jokes.punchline: Catherine sincerely asks what she's actually done to help. Selina has no answer and stammers.
jokes.explanation: A quietly devastating joke about Selina's relationship with her daughter — the platitude exposed as hollow in real time. The cringe comes from Catherine's guileless sincerity forcing Selina into an uncomfortable truth. Strong character integration for both.

jokes.text: Jonah describes the meme of him 'fucking a chicken while dressed as bin Laden' and the debate over who is wearing the bin Laden costume.
jokes.setup: Jonah reports that he's getting online hate for denouncing the internet.
jokes.punchline: Dan asks 'The chicken is dressed as bin Laden or you're dressed as bin Laden?' — the most specific question possible about an already absurd scenario. Jonah clarifies he's dressed as bin Laden, then notes it's 'really well done' and 'legitimately looks' like bestiality while insulting 9/11 victims.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's peaks. The joke escalates in layers: (1) the initial absurd image, (2) Dan's pedantic need to clarify which party is in costume, (3) Jonah's resignation that it's 'really well done,' (4) the clinical summary that it involves bestiality AND 9/11 insult simultaneously. Each beat is funnier than the last. Darkly original and perfectly structured. The escalation from 'online hate' to 'legitimately looks like I'm engaging in bestiality while insulting 9/11 victims' is a masterclass in comedic escalation.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I'm like a boom op on a porn shoot right now.' Dan: 'I can't have a terrorist chicken-fucker next to the president. Security risk.'
jokes.setup: Jonah wants a better office closer to the action; Dan refuses.
jokes.punchline: Dan's stated reason for refusal: 'I can't have a terrorist chicken-fucker next to the president. Security risk.' — treating the internet meme as a legitimate security consideration.
jokes.explanation: 'Terrorist chicken-fucker' as a hyphenated compound noun applied seriously as a security designation is extraordinary. Dan treating internet meme content as grounds for an actual security clearance decision is the perfect capper to the escalation. Callback to the meme description elevates it further.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I'm like a boom op on a porn shoot right now.'
jokes.setup: Jonah complains about being too far from the action in his West Wing posting.
jokes.punchline: The boom op metaphor — technically on set but nowhere near the main event — is an oddly perfect and self-aware analogy from Jonah.
jokes.explanation: Surprisingly apt metaphor from Jonah — the boom op image is specific and correct. It's funnier that he chose a porn shoot specifically. Good economy.

jokes.text: Gary has been carrying special shoes for Selina since a New York promise years ago — she doesn't remember making the promise.
jokes.setup: Gary produces the shoes he's been carrying for years, waiting for this historic moment.
jokes.punchline: Selina clearly doesn't remember making the promise. She fakes recognition, then laughs nervously. Gary is oblivious to her total blankness.
jokes.explanation: A quietly devastating Gary moment. His devotion has him carrying shoes for years based on an offhand comment Selina has completely forgotten. The cringe comes from his complete sincerity and her completely empty performance of remembering. Perfect character integration for the Gary-Selina dynamic.

jokes.text: Selina: 'This is like Kathy Bates in Misery.' (about Gary putting the shoes on her)
jokes.setup: Gary is putting the special shoes on Selina's feet in a ceremonial, Prince Charming-esque way.
jokes.punchline: Selina compares the moment — which Gary finds touching — to the obsessive kidnapper in 'Misery,' suggesting his devotion is frightening rather than sweet.
jokes.explanation: 'Kathy Bates in Misery' is a precise reference that nails Gary's character from Selina's POV. The fact that Gary is too devoted to be offended by it makes the cringe land harder. Strong character integration — Selina's ambivalence about Gary's devotion is crystallized perfectly.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Yeah, except there's no bridesmaids to fuck.' (about the inauguration being like a wedding)
jokes.setup: Mike says the inauguration is exciting, 'like a wedding or something.'
jokes.punchline: Dan immediately identifies the only thing missing from the wedding analogy: no bridesmaids to sleep with.
jokes.explanation: Pure Dan — his mind immediately goes to sexual opportunity even in historic political moments. Strong character integration. The joke itself isn't novel but the timing (immediately after a wholesome sentiment) is well-executed.

jokes.text: Selina's shoes squeak loudly throughout the entire inaugural address. Visual/audio gag: every step produces a loud mouse-toy squeak.
jokes.setup: Gary has produced special shoes for Selina's inauguration. Selina puts them on and walks to the podium.
jokes.punchline: The shoes squeak loudly and continuously — like dog toys — throughout the inaugural address, undermining the solemnity of the moment completely.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best constructed jokes. The setup is long and invested — Gary's devotion, the special shoes, the historic moment — which makes the payoff devastating. The shoes squeaking like dog toys through the PRESIDENTIAL INAUGURAL ADDRESS is a comedic catastrophe perfectly calibrated. The slow fade of squeaking as Selina stops moving ('squeaking slows, stops') before the speech then resuming is beautifully structured. This is earned comedy: Gary's well-meaning devotion becomes a comedic weapon. Earned vs. cheap scores very high because all the ingredients were placed in advance.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Jesus Christ, Gary. Are those real shoes or are those fucking dog toys?' Aide: 'Sounds like the theme from Psycho.'
jokes.setup: The shoes are squeaking loudly.
jokes.punchline: Dan's confusion about whether they're shoes or dog toys, plus the Psycho theme comparison — which both insult Gary and add absurdist horror-movie framing to the situation.
jokes.explanation: The Psycho theme comparison is inspired — it reframes the squeaking as horror-movie foreshadowing, which is accurate given what the shoes are about to do to the inauguration. Dog toys line is simpler but effective.

jokes.text: Selina delivers her inaugural address while aides are loudly weeping and checking Twitter. 'Are you seeing this on Twitter?' / 'Yup. President squeaks to the nation.'
jokes.setup: Selina delivers her inaugural address with the squeaky shoes.
jokes.punchline: An aide is live-checking Twitter during the speech and reading out the trending mockery — 'President squeaks to the nation' — while Selina speaks.
jokes.explanation: The cringe of checking Twitter during the inaugural address is compounded by reading aloud the mockery while Selina is still speaking. A layer of humiliation added to an already excruciating moment. Good escalation.

jokes.text: Gary weeps audibly during Selina's inaugural address. Selina shoots him an irritated look mid-speech.
jokes.setup: Selina is delivering her inaugural address — the most important speech of her career — while Gary stands nearby.
jokes.punchline: Gary's emotional weeping interrupts the speech enough for Selina to have to shoot him a look, mid-presidential-address, as if scolding a child.
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy that perfectly encapsulates the Gary/Selina dynamic: his love for her is so overwhelming it creates professional disasters; her management of him mid-speech ('Oh, my God, you know better than that') while still being president is hilarious. The maternal scolding during a presidential address is perfectly Veep.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Take these fucking shoes and shoot 'em in the fucking head.' / 'I am the most powerful person in the world. Is that correct?' / 'Yes, ma'am.' / 'Any fuckup from now on is not just a fuckup. It's my legacy. Got it? No fuckups.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just delivered her inaugural address to squeaking shoes and public ridicule.
jokes.punchline: She pivots from shoe-murder instructions to a presidential declaration about her legacy — the tonal whiplash from 'shoot 'em in the fucking head' to 'it's my legacy' in the same breath.
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash is the joke — shoe execution orders immediately followed by solemn presidential declarations about legacy. Selina's character is perfectly captured: she can pivot from unhinged rage to presidential gravitas in one breath. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: The Leslie Kerr/Leslie Carr/Leanne Carr name confusion chain — Selina fires the wrong person entirely.
jokes.setup: Selina told someone to fire 'Leslie Carr' from Energy. She meant 'Leanne Carr.' The order was interpreted as 'Leslie Kerr' from State.
jokes.punchline: The wrong person was fired, the Iranians are furious, and the name chain unfolds: Leslie Carr → Leslie Kerr → Leanne Carr — three wrong names, one diplomatic crisis.
jokes.explanation: A cascading bureaucratic disaster caused by mishearing a name — perfectly Veep in its satirical precision. The joke is that the entire chain of failure is rooted in Selina's throwaway joke about energy levels. The structure is immaculate: setup (the Carr joke earlier), revelation of wrong name, escalation to international incident. Also pays off Selina's earlier joke about Leslie Carr not having energy.

jokes.text: Mike: It was a mishearing which led to a misspeaking which led to a misfiring.
jokes.setup: Mike is explaining the Leslie Kerr mistake to the press.
jokes.punchline: The triple 'mis-' construction — mishearing, misspeaking, misfiring — is a neat verbal escalation that also describes an actual foreign policy crisis as a grammar lesson.
jokes.explanation: The triple alliterative construction is genuinely clever and specific — the 'mis-' prefix chain is elegant and mirrors the domino structure of the actual error. It's also a very Mike thing to say: accidentally making a catastrophe sound like a tongue-twister. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Ben: 'You might as well drive a suicide clown car into the fucking Lincoln Memorial.'
jokes.setup: The team is discussing the PR implications of trying to walk back the Leslie Kerr firing.
jokes.punchline: Ben's hyperbole reaches a peak with the image of a 'suicide clown car' driven into the Lincoln Memorial — an image that combines political, vehicular, and circus disasters simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: An extraordinary piece of political insult imagery. 'Suicide clown car' is itself a brilliant compound — it implies both catastrophic failure (suicide) and absurd incompetence (clown car) — and hitting the Lincoln Memorial puts it squarely in Washington iconography. This is Ben at his most colorfully nihilistic. The image is original, vivid, and deeply quotable. Justifies 8+ because the specific combination is genuinely novel and the imagery is precise in its comedic logic.

jokes.text: Selina: 'What man is named Leslie, for fuck's sakes?' Ben/Mike: 'Leslie Nielsen. Leslie Moonves. Leslie Frazier, the coach for the Vikings...'
jokes.setup: Selina dismisses the name Leslie as implausibly male.
jokes.punchline: Ben and Mike immediately rattle off a list of famous men named Leslie, including notably the coach for the Vikings — an oddly specific entry that escalates to absurdity.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from Nielsen (famous) to Moonves (media insider) to 'Leslie Frazier, coach for the Vikings' (extremely obscure) is the joke — the increasingly desperate specificity of the list. 'Doesn't matter' from Selina is the perfect brushoff.

jokes.text: Kent lists men named Leslie — Leslie Nielsen, Leslie Moonves, Leslie Frazier the Vikings coach. / Selina: Doesn't matter. You're... good speech.
jokes.setup: Selina complains 'What man is named Leslie, for fuck's sake?'
jokes.punchline: Kent earnestly begins listing men named Leslie with statistical precision, until Selina dismisses him with 'Doesn't matter. You're... good speech.'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kent character moment — he literally cannot not answer a factual question, even in the middle of a political crisis. The comedy is in his complete inability to read the room, and in Selina's deflated 'Doesn't matter. You're... good speech.' — which is itself funny for being grammatically broken and insincere. The 'Vikings coach' specificity is perfect Kent. Earns 8+ for character precision.

jokes.text: Selina: 'He's useless. He's a one-inch cock.' (about Secretary Maddox)
jokes.setup: Secretary Maddox requests an urgent meeting.
jokes.punchline: Selina's instant verdict: 'He's useless. He's a one-inch cock.' — the anatomical insult calibrated for maximum dismissiveness.
jokes.explanation: Functional Selina profanity — the specific anatomical measurement adds precision to the insult. Not especially original in Veep's universe of insults, but delivered with characteristic confidence.

jokes.text: Reporter: 'President Meyer is making Iran irate. Imagine what she could do in 24 hours. Imagine Selina Meyer is the only person keeping your children safe at night. 'Cause she is.'
jokes.setup: Coverage of Selina's Iran gaffe two hours into her presidency.
jokes.punchline: The reporter's terrifying pivot from mockery to 'she IS the only person keeping your children safe at night' — acknowledging the existential horror of the situation they're laughing at.
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire — the commentator can't help acknowledging that their subject of mockery is also their actual president with actual nuclear codes. The tonal shift from pundit snarking to existential dread is very Veep.

jokes.text: Mike at press briefing: 'I mean, we all make mistakes... just ask your mom and dad.' (laughs) / 'Has President Meyer spoken to Iran?' 'I'm not able to answer that. Otherwise I'd have to have you killed.' (laughs)
jokes.setup: Mike is handling press questions about the Leslie Kerr firing crisis.
jokes.punchline: Mike makes two 'jokes' — one about the reporters' parents making mistakes, one about killing reporters — both received in silence or with nervous laughter, revealing his complete inability to read the room.
jokes.explanation: The 'otherwise I'd have to have you killed' is a classic press secretary bad-joke that lands with the cringe of someone who thinks he's doing well under pressure. Mike using humor to deflect a serious crisis while clearly bombing is excellent character work.

jokes.text: Richard Splett: 'I think they were deprived of fresh air at birth.' (about rude callers)
jokes.setup: Splett and Kelly explain why they took a break from phone banking — callers were rude.
jokes.punchline: Splett's explanation for voter rudeness: 'deprived of fresh air at birth' — a very specific, medically-adjacent non-sequitur delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Pure Splett — bizarre, specific, earnest pseudo-medical diagnosis of voter behavior. His character's verbal tics (strange literal-mindedness) shine through this odd non-sequitur.

jokes.text: Kent: 'They only got one vote left in 'em, we need it.' / 'Warm, upright bodies that can still punch a hole in a card.'
jokes.setup: The team is discussing the value of Jeff Kane's AARP senior citizen voter bloc.
jokes.punchline: Kent describes elderly voters as 'warm, upright bodies that can still punch a hole in a card' — reducing them to the minimum biological requirements for casting a ballot.
jokes.explanation: One of Kent's best lines in the episode. 'Warm, upright bodies that can still punch a hole in a card' is devastatingly precise — reducing democratic participation to its lowest biological threshold. The word 'upright' (not dead) and 'still' (barely) add clinical precision to the dark comedy. Economy is excellent — every word does work. Justifies 8+ because it's genuinely original, sharply structured, and perfectly in Kent's robotic data-analyst voice.

jokes.text: Amy: 'Kent, your rhetoric, as always, is inspiring.'
jokes.setup: Kent has just described elderly voters as 'warm, upright bodies.'
jokes.punchline: Amy's deadpan 'inspiring' — pure sarcasm, perfectly timed.
jokes.explanation: Functional reaction beat. Amy's sarcasm is well-placed and in character, but it's a straight read of 'I'm being sarcastic' that doesn't add much beyond the reaction to Kent's line.

jokes.text: Amy: 'He's got old people coming out of his ass.'
jokes.setup: The team discusses Jeff Kane's AARP connections.
jokes.punchline: Amy's colorful description of Kane's reach into the elderly voter demographic.
jokes.explanation: Functional vulgarity — the image is funny but not especially original. Amy's exasperated directness is well-characterized but this is one of her weaker lines.

jokes.text: Jonah's negotiation demands: West Exec parking for the Cube, mess hall privileges, and 'somebody that works for me — a Jonah.' / 'And henceforth they will no longer be known as Jonahs. They'll be known as Jimmys or Pepes or Sarahs or whatever the fuck that person's name turns out to be. And you can't hire anyone named Jonah.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is negotiating his terms for delivering his Uncle Jeff's voter bloc.
jokes.punchline: Jonah knows he's historically been the 'fetch-it' of the office — so much so that the role has been named after him ('Jonahs'). His demand is to have his own 'Jonah,' and to rebrand the role, while specifically prohibiting anyone named Jonah from filling it.
jokes.explanation: Outstanding character comedy. The revelation that Jonah's name has become a generic noun for a low-status errand runner is both funny and devastating. His demand to have his own 'Jonah' and rebrand the role (while knowing you can't name them Jonah — he's thought this through) shows both his self-awareness and complete lack of self-awareness simultaneously. The escalating specificity of 'Jimmys or Pepes or Sarahs or whatever the fuck' is perfectly Jonah. Justifies 8+ on character integration alone — no one else could deliver this.

jokes.text: Dan: 'Maybe Chung didn't torture that guy, but he sure is waterboarding the fuck out of us in the polls.'
jokes.setup: The team is flying back to New Hampshire amid the bad poll news about Chung.
jokes.punchline: 'Waterboarding the fuck out of us in the polls' — extending the torture metaphor to their electoral suffering.
jokes.explanation: Very sharp callback wordplay — taking the Chung torture storyline and applying it as a polling metaphor is economical and clever. 'Waterboarding the fuck out of us' as a political verb is memorable and exactly Dan's register.

jokes.text: Mike: 'Yeah, like a Snapchat presidency.' (about Selina potentially having the shortest administration in history)
jokes.setup: Selina fears coming in third could end her presidency almost immediately.
jokes.punchline: Mike's analogy: a 'Snapchat presidency' — disappearing after a few seconds.
jokes.explanation: Clever tech analogy that works well for its era. 'Snapchat presidency' captures the brevity with a single cultural reference. Mike's line, which is unusually pithy for him, gets a mild laugh.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Yeah, can we fly it into Danny Chung?' (about Air Force One)
jokes.setup: Selina remarks on how amazing the presidential plane is.
jokes.punchline: Ben's immediate suggestion: weaponize it against their primary opponent.
jokes.explanation: Ben's nihilistic suggestion to crash the presidential aircraft into a political rival is delivered with such exhausted matter-of-factness that it lands as character comedy more than shock. Good economy — the image is outrageous but the delivery is bone-dry.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Yeah, everybody wants to fuck 'em, too. God, I would love to fuck a firefighter. Hey, I'm the president. I can fuck anybody I want now, right? All the other ones have.'
jokes.setup: The team is discussing the photo op at a firefighter equipment factory. Someone notes everyone loves firefighters.
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating presidential sexual entitlement: from 'everyone wants to fuck firefighters' to 'I'm the president, I can fuck anybody I want' to 'all the other ones have' — a devastating comment on presidential conduct.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from general firefighter attraction to 'I'm the president' entitlement to the devastating 'all the other ones have' (presidential sexual history implied) is perfectly built. Selina's power-drunk sexual entitlement mirroring historical male presidents is sharp political satire with great economy.

jokes.text: Selina: Yeah, everybody wants to fuck 'em, too. God, I would love to fuck a firefighter. Hey, I'm the president. I can fuck anybody I want now, right? All the other ones have.
jokes.setup: Gary says 'everybody loves firefighters, everybody wants to keep them safe.'
jokes.punchline: Selina's pivot from 'keep them safe' to wanting to sleep with one, then using presidential precedent as justification ('All the other ones have').
jokes.explanation: Excellent sequence. Each beat escalates: wanting to have sex with a firefighter → presidential entitlement → historical precedent. 'All the other ones have' is a sharp political observation (JFK, Clinton, etc.) delivered as a casual non sequitur. Very Selina in her combination of vanity, entitlement, and self-awareness.

jokes.text: Aide: 'She is so good at making people believe she is good with people.' / 'She's on fire.' (barely audible chiming in background)
jokes.setup: Selina is schmoozing workers at the factory, asking about firefighter suits.
jokes.punchline: 'She is so good at making people believe she is good with people' — an observation that perfectly summarizes political performance while being a devastating indictment.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional piece of political observation. The recursive structure — good at making people believe she's good — captures the entire performance-politics paradox in one sentence. Highly quotable as political commentary. The 'she's on fire' line then earning an unintended irony about the fire safety equipment context is a nice bonus.

jokes.text: Dan: 'A great advantage that she has over the other candidates in this race is that she's the president.' Aide: 'She's not the president.'
jokes.setup: Dan is talking up Selina's advantages as they watch her at the factory photo op.
jokes.punchline: The aide flatly corrects him: 'She's not the president' — because the oath was botched and she technically isn't yet.
jokes.explanation: Clean callback that lands well. Three words of correction demolishing Dan's confident spin is a good structural joke. The absurdity of 'she's the president' being technically incorrect despite all evidence is Veep's wheelhouse.

jokes.text: Selina gets the oath wrong because Mike fell into a lamp during the ceremony.
jokes.setup: The oath of office — the most solemn moment of a presidency — is being administered.
jokes.punchline: Mike falls into a lamp during the administration of the oath, interrupting Selina mid-word and causing her to get 'preserve' wrong.
jokes.explanation: Mike falling into a lamp at the exact wrong moment — nullifying a presidential oath — is inspired. The consequences cascade through the rest of the episode (second swearing-in, New Hampshire photo op, the whole thing). A perfectly escalating piece of physical comedy that has maximum narrative consequence. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Get a local judge to do it.' Amy: 'Wait, isn't that gonna look just a little bit tacky?' Ben: 'That could be really great for us. Play up to New Hampshire's already inflated sense of self-importance.'
jokes.setup: Selina needs to retake the botched oath in New Hampshire. Options are debated.
jokes.punchline: Ben immediately reframes 'tacky' as a political asset — using New Hampshire's 'inflated sense of self-importance' to spin a screwup as a compliment.
jokes.explanation: Ben's cynical political instinct to turn a disaster into a compliment for a voter bloc is sharp. 'New Hampshire's already inflated sense of self-importance' is a precise and funny regional dig. Good political satire.

jokes.text: Ben/Amy: 'Isn't that gonna look just a little bit tacky? / I don't think about it. / No, no, no. That could be really great for us. Play up to New Hampshire's already inflated sense of self-importance.'
jokes.setup: The team is debating whether to get a local judge to do a second swearing-in rather than returning to DC
jokes.punchline: Amy's spin reframes 'tacky' as 'playing up to New Hampshire's already inflated sense of self-importance' — a precise, contemptuous reading of state political culture
jokes.explanation: 'New Hampshire's already inflated sense of self-importance' is a sharp piece of political satire about primary-state entitlement. The pivot from 'tacky' to 'strategic opportunity' in one beat is good Amy character work.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Get Catherine, okay? 'Cause I gotta... you know... have her hold the thing.' All aides: 'The Bible.'
jokes.setup: Selina is organizing a second swearing-in ceremony.
jokes.punchline: She can't remember the word 'Bible' — all her aides finish her sentence in unison with 'The Bible.'
jokes.explanation: Good group reaction beat — the synchronized 'The Bible' suggests this is not the first time they've had to finish her sentences. Selina not knowing the word 'Bible' during her own presidential swearing-in is nicely absurd.

jokes.text: Ben: 'Iran is officially pissed that you fired Leslie Kerr. Apparently they liked the guy. It must be the beard.'
jokes.setup: Iran has made an official statement about the Leslie Kerr firing.
jokes.punchline: Ben's diplomatic analysis: 'It must be the beard' — reducing a geopolitical crisis to a matter of facial hair aesthetics.
jokes.explanation: Ben's deflation of Iranian geopolitics to beard appreciation is perfectly economical. 'It must be the beard' is a four-word punchline that suggests both Middle Eastern facial hair stereotypes and Ben's exhausted cynicism about international relations.

jokes.text: Ben: Apparently they liked the guy. It must be the beard.
jokes.setup: Iran is upset that Selina fired Leslie Kerr, who Iran liked.
jokes.punchline: Ben's explanation for why Iran liked a US diplomat — 'must be the beard' — is offhanded and possibly accurate (beard = Muslim respect context), delivered with zero elaboration.
jokes.explanation: Ben's breezy dismissal of a diplomatic crisis to a grooming choice is perfectly in character. The possible cultural accuracy (beards signifying respect) makes it slightly layered.

jokes.text: Ben: 'So you'd rather piss off blue-collar truck drivers and their chicken-frying wives? Shh. We're surrounded by these people. Keep your voice down.'
jokes.setup: Ben argues for reinstating Leslie Kerr to placate Iran, even if it's bad PR. The debate continues at the factory.
jokes.punchline: Ben is about to deliver a classist/regional generalization about working-class voters when he suddenly realizes they're literally surrounded by those exact people and shushes himself mid-sentence.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured cringe joke. Ben's political contempt for voters expressed openly, then the immediate self-correction when he realizes context — the 'shh, we're surrounded by these people' — captures the essential cynicism of political operatives perfectly. The fact that he doesn't stop the insult, just tries to muffle it, is the extra comedic layer. Strong structure: insult → realization → attempted damage control.

jokes.text: Jonah to Jeff Kane: 'I am just honored to be a part of your team. And I will serve you faithfully as long as you happen to be president.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is being introduced to Jeff Kane as part of the deal for his uncle's AARP voter support.
jokes.punchline: 'As long as you happen to be president' — Jonah's loyalty pledge has an explicit expiry clause that he delivers to Selina's face as if it's a compliment.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Jonah — completely unable to give a compliment without an escape clause, and entirely unaware that 'as long as you happen to be president' is a devastating qualifier. The conditionality of his praise makes it an insult he can't see. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Catherine: I mean, I like this one better than the first one. / Aide: This looks ordinary, it looks relatable. / Staffer: It looks horrible. She looks like she's in 'Flashdance.'
jokes.setup: Selina's second swearing-in is taking place in a random factory office with bad lighting.
jokes.punchline: Three responses to the optics — Catherine's naive enthusiasm, an aide's spin, and a staffer's honest 'Flashdance' comparison.
jokes.explanation: The three-perspective setup delivers an escalating response: naive → spin → brutal honesty. 'Flashdance' is a specific, evocative comparison that works well.

jokes.text: Second swearing-in at the factory: aides debate photography, calling it 'Flashdance' and joking 'Thai lady-boy made head of sweatshop.'
jokes.setup: Selina is being sworn in again at the firefighter equipment factory. Aides are watching and commenting.
jokes.punchline: The aides' commentary on the swearing-in photos: it looks like Flashdance, and then the shockingly offensive 'Thai lady-boy made head of sweatshop' — a deeply inappropriate photo caption imagined by political operatives.
jokes.explanation: The Flashdance reference is sharp (the factory setting does evoke it), but 'Thai lady-boy' veers into a type of offensive that feels cheap rather than sharp satire. The Veep writers usually make prejudice the target of the joke, but here the transphobic/xenophobic phrasing is somewhat lazy. Docked on earned vs. cheap.

jokes.text: Catherine: 'I think there were fundamental issues with the New Hampshire campaign and I just wish I'd have been here earlier to advise you.' Mike: 'Campaigns are a lot more difficult than experimental dance troupes, Catherine.'
jokes.setup: Catherine is offering her post-mortem on the New Hampshire results.
jokes.punchline: Mike's takedown: 'Campaigns are a lot more difficult than experimental dance troupes, Catherine' — suggesting that Catherine's previous domain of expertise is interpretive dance.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Mike comeback — unusually sharp for him, which itself is funny. 'Experimental dance troupes' is delightfully specific as Catherine's imagined prior experience, and it lands a solid put-down while revealing the character dynamic between Mike and Selina's artsy daughter.

jokes.text: Mike: Campaigns are a lot more difficult than experimental dance troupes, Catherine.
jokes.setup: Catherine suggests she could have helped the New Hampshire campaign. Mike dismisses her.
jokes.punchline: The very specific 'experimental dance troupes' implies Catherine's previous experience/interests, and Mike's condescension is pitch-perfect.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'experimental dance troupes' does enormous comedic work — it tells us everything about Catherine and how the team sees her, while Mike's delivery is perfectly condescending. You don't need to know Catherine well for this to land; the choice of 'experimental dance' as the comparative standard is inspired. Earns 8+ for character precision and economy.

jokes.text: Mike: 'It's good we won Dixville Notch.' Dan: 'It's got a population of 12, Mike.' Mike: 'I know, Dan, but it's famous.' Dan: 'Our Lord Jesus started with 12.' Mike: 'He didn't win New Hampshire, either.'
jokes.setup: The team is anxiously watching New Hampshire results. Mike grasps at positives.
jokes.punchline: The Jesus comparison escalates beautifully: Dan invokes Christ starting with 12 disciples as a joke, but Mike's literal comeback — 'He didn't win New Hampshire, either' — is the perfect final blow.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's absolute best jokes. The structure is impeccable: Mike's desperate spin (Dixville Notch!) → Dan's deflation (12 people) → Mike's Bible defense → Dan's Jesus comparison (setup) → Mike's literal punchline that Jesus didn't win New Hampshire either. The final line works because it's both accurate (Jesus didn't run in NH) and devastatingly bathetic — reducing Christ's ministry to a primary loss. Economy is near-perfect. Highly quotable. Justifies 8+ because the construction is exactly as crisp as the calibration anchors.

jokes.text: Selina comes in third in New Hampshire. A photo is taken of her devastated expression. She says: 'Delete that.'
jokes.setup: Selina gets the news she came in third in New Hampshire.
jokes.punchline: Her response to the devastating result is not about the result — it's 'delete that' about the photo of her reaction. Vanity overrides grief.
jokes.explanation: Two words of perfect character comedy. Selina Meyer's entire character is encapsulated in 'Delete that' — image management as her first instinct even in genuine crisis. Maximum economy (ten on economy is rare; this earns it). The reaction beat of the photo being taken at the worst possible moment, followed by her immediate vanity overriding everything, is expertly structured. Justifies 8+ because 'Delete that' functions identically to the calibration anchors — two words that reveal a full character.

jokes.text: Unnamed aide: 'It's one percent. That's nothing. All second in our eyes, I'll tell you that much.'
jokes.setup: Selina has come in third by one percent. An aide tries to put a positive spin on it.
jokes.punchline: 'All second in our eyes' — a completely delusional attempt to spin third place as second.
jokes.explanation: Functional political spin comedy — the aide's denial of mathematical reality is mildly funny but not memorable. The execution doesn't have enough specificity to stand out.

jokes.text: Dan to Jonah (who wants a desk by the window): 'Yeah, Dan? Promise to jump out of it?' Jonah: 'Well, I'd be on the first floor, so that really wouldn't work, would it?'
jokes.setup: Jonah asks for a desk by the window.
jokes.punchline: Dan suggests he'd jump out of it (as in, commit suicide from boredom/misery). Jonah's response takes the suggestion literally and rules it out on practical grounds — not offended, just logistical.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jonah character joke. He doesn't process the insult as an insult — he processes it as a practical suggestion and evaluates it on its merits (it wouldn't work: first floor). His literal-minded response reveals both his obliviousness and, paradoxically, a kind of unshakeable self-possession.

jokes.text: Jonah: Well, I'd be on the first floor, so that really wouldn't work, would it?
jokes.setup: Dan suggests Jonah wants the window to jump out of it.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's literal, practical response — the window is on the first floor, so the jump wouldn't be lethal — delivered as if he's genuinely considered and dismissed the idea.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's response is funnier than Dan's setup — he engages with the suicide suggestion literally and pragmatically, which is both absurd and very Jonah. The matter-of-fact 'really wouldn't work, would it?' implies he's thought it through.

jokes.text: Amy: 'Don't get too concerned about New Hampshire, ma'am.' Selina: 'I came in third, Amy. Okay? Even the Nazis came in second.'
jokes.setup: Amy tries to reassure Selina about coming in third in New Hampshire.
jokes.punchline: Selina's devastating counter: 'Even the Nazis came in second' — invoking history's most notorious political organization to make the point that even they outperformed her tonight.
jokes.explanation: The best joke of the episode and one of the finest lines in Veep's run. The structure is near-perfect: Amy's hopeful platitude sets up Selina's despair, and Selina doesn't reach for just any comparison — she reaches for the Nazis, specifically to note they at least came in second. The historical accuracy (Nazi party did rise to prominence before gaining power) doesn't matter — what matters is the audacity of using the Holocaust's architects as a benchmark for electoral performance. The line encapsulates Selina's vanity (she's more upset about losing than the evil comparison), her political ruthlessness, and the show's willingness to go anywhere for a joke. Maximum quotability — this line becomes the episode's defining moment and travels far outside its context. Near the calibration ceiling of 9.5 for the best TV jokes ever written.

tmdb_overview: Three days before the New Hampshire primary, Selina and her staff juggle her multiple official obligations and campaign appearances.
insight: Season finale deploys 66 jokes in 38 minutes—Veep's densest stretch yet.
